story:
Tamsin squints at sunlight dancing on the river, hunger pricking as she thinks of the feast to come—pink-fleshed salmon, summer heat preserved in every bite. Mika finds her on the river stones, his newly honed muscles taut beneath his hide leggings, and the familiar closeness sets a flush in her cheeks. He tucks a strand of black hair behind her ear. They tease, laugh, and sit shoulder to shoulder, and in Tamsin’s chest a steady certainty grows: tonight, after the feast, she will ask him to be hers.

In their village, women choose their husbands. Tamsin has waited, letting time grow them into who they should be. Mika seeks her out in crowds, finds reasons for knees to touch, looks at her sometimes like a hunter, other times like a friend holding shared memories up to the sun. She is sure this is love.

They sit together at the feast, sharing fish and berries while elders’ knowing glances slide across them. Tamsin can no longer hold back. She asks Mika to walk with her along the forest trail to the field of sweetgrass at the river’s bend, hidden from the village. The stars scatter light through the canopy. Her ochre-starred dress brushes ferns as her heart gallops. In the sweetgrass, she will ask; if he says yes, she will kiss him until the first birds sing.

Thunder rumbles. Lightning flashes. Before she can speak, Mika’s shout cuts the night. An orange glow blooms behind them—the camp is on fire. They race back. Lightning has struck the reed dwellings; flames climb roofs and leap to thin, dry trees. Fear eases when Tamsin sees the people calm, organized—then shatters as Reep screams for her baby. Utka stands alone inside a ring of fire, new to walking, barely steady, too far for any mother’s reach.

Mika is through the fire in a breath, scooping the child and carrying her to safety. At the edge of the trees he stops, kneels, and gently sets Utka down before she toddles to Reep. Only then does Tamsin remember: Mika’s breath often fails him after a sprint. He falls to his back, gasping. A fire-weakened branch cracks above and drops with a heavy thunk across his chest. Flames roar. Tamsin runs, shields her face, and heaves the burning branch from Mika’s body. Her palms blister, but she will not let fire steal his breath.

Healers visit daily. Mika’s breathing is shallow, his body motionless, the bruise blooming deep purple across his ribs. Tamsin abandons every duty to sit by him, feeding sips of broth, counting slow heartbeats. A moon cycle turns. Her hope thins like smoke. At night, with her ear on his chest, she hears a farewell drum. She prays—strength for him, strength for herself—and feels ancestors gather, watchful, undeciding.

Morning filters through reed walls. The scent of fern and smoke lingers on Mika’s clothes, a thread back to what might have been. His breath is barely there. Tamsin leans close. “Mika,” she whispers, voice breaking, “will you be mine?” Silence pools. She cradles him, presses a tender farewell kiss to his dry, soft lips.

He coughs. His lashes flutter, eyes finding her. With effort, he swallows and rasps, “Yes. Yes, of course I will be yours.” A tiny smile lifts one corner of his mouth.

Relief floods Tamsin. In her mind she sees their children—one with Mika’s dimple, one with her long legs—splashing at the river’s edge. The feast, the fire, the vigil, and the prayer fold into a single truth: her Mika will live, and their future, once scorched, begins to green again.
